channel.title: NYT > Most Recent Headlines
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Neandertales hacían endodoncias hace 59.000 años
item.description: Los homínidos prehistóricos “aparentemente eran muy hábiles en lo que consideraríamos medicina invasiva”, afirmó el antropólogo John Olsen.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz

item.title: NYT Spelling Bee Answers for May 20, 2026
item.description: Feeling stuck on today’s puzzle? We can help.
item.dc:creator: New York Times Games

item.title: Manhattan D.A. Is Urged to Pursue a Criminal Case Against Eric Adams
item.description: Citizens Union, a government watchdog group, suggested that although Mr. Adams’s federal indictment was dropped, his alleged offenses would still be state crimes.
item.dc:creator: Dana Rubinstein, Hurubie Meko, William K. Rashbaum and Jonah E. Bromwich
item.category: Eric Adams Federal Corruption Case (24 CR 556)

media:content.media:credit: Olga Fedorova for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Federal prosecutors charged Mr. Adams with bribery, soliciting illegal campaign donations and defrauding the city’s campaign finance program in 2024.

item.title: Doctors Asked Officers to Unshackle a Patient. They Refused for 26 Days.
item.description: A lawsuit challenges the police practice of shackling mentally ill arrestees in New York, sometimes for long periods, while they await arraignment in locked psychiatric wards.
item.dc:creator: Andy Newman

item.title: Google renueva su barra de búsqueda tras 25 años
item.description: Con un nuevo modelo de inteligencia artificial Gemini, la compañía tecnológica modifica las dimensiones de su barra de búsqueda para atender consultas más extensas, incorpora una herramienta de generación de video y facilita las compras por internet.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle, Kate Conger and Brian X. Chen

media:content.media:credit: Jason Henry para The New York Times
media:content.media:description: El martes, Google dijo que el cambio hacia la inteligencia artificial la había inspirado a renovar su barra de búsqueda por primera vez desde 2001.

item.title: China Confirms It Will Buy 200 Boeing Planes
item.description: The deal, which President Trump announced after last week’s summit, represents the largest single sale of the American company’s aircraft to Beijing in nearly a decade.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson

media:content.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: President Trump attending a state banquet with China’s top leader, Xi Jinping, in Beijing last week.

item.title: Late Night Could Set a Clock by Trump’s Iran Attack Delays
item.description: Jimmy Kimmel noted that the president backs down on his own deadlines to bomb Iran “every Tuesday. It’s like a new episode of ‘NCIS.’”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Palabra del día en inglés: ‘marvelous’
item.description: Esta palabra ha aparecido en 128 artículos en NYTimes.com en el último año. ¿Puedes usarla en una frase?
item.dc:creator: The Learning Network

item.title: Word of the Day: marvelous
item.description: This word has appeared in 128 articles on NYTimes.com in the past year. Can you use it in a sentence?
item.dc:creator: The Learning Network

item.title: Picture Prompt: ‘Lowkey’
item.description: What do you think when adults use teen slang?
item.dc:creator: The Learning Network

item.title: I’m the Foreign Minister of Sweden. Don’t Overestimate Russia.
item.description: We can’t afford to underestimate Russia’s strength and its ability to harm Ukraine and NATO allies, but we must not overestimate it either.
item.dc:creator: Maria Malmer Stenergard

item.title: South Carolina House Passes New Map Aimed at Forcing Out Clyburn
item.description: The changes include a redraw of the congressional district held by James E. Clyburn, the Democratic power broker. The map now goes to the State Senate.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina

item.title: Georgia Supreme Court Justices Fend Off Left-Leaning Challengers
item.description: The Republican-appointed incumbents had faced well-financed opponents who infused issues like abortion rights into the nonpartisan elections.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas

media:content.media:credit: Kate Brumback/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: The Nathan Deal Judicial Center in Atlanta, home of the state’s Supreme Court and its Court of Appeals.

item.title: Así es como Irán ganó influencia en la guerra
item.description: Al verse superado militarmente, Irán recurrió a la “coacción triangular” atacando a los Estados del Golfo y cerrando el estrecho de Ormuz. Esto pone de manifiesto una vulnerabilidad a largo plazo de Estados Unidos.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Taub
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Una valla publicitaria en la plaza Enghelab de Teherán, en abril, que mostraba material militar estadounidense atrapado en una red.

item.title: El objetivo inicial de la guerra en Irán era instalar al expresidente como líder
item.description: Un ataque israelí para liberar a Mahmud Ahmadineyad de su arresto domiciliario en Teherán, dijeron funcionarios estadounidenses, formaba parte de un intento de incitar un cambio de régimen.
item.dc:creator: Mark Mazzetti, Julian E. Barnes, Farnaz Fassihi and Ronen Bergman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris para The New York Times
media:content.media:description: El expresidente iraní Mahmud Ahmadineyad se inscribe como candidato a las elecciones presidenciales de 2024 en Teherán.

item.title: How Iran Gained Leverage in the War
item.description: Outmatched militarily, Iran used “triangular coercion” by attacking Gulf states and closing the Strait of Hormuz. It points to a long-term U.S. vulnerability.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Taub
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: A billboard in Enghelab Square in Tehran in April showing American military hardware trapped in a net.

item.title: Corrections: May 20, 2026
item.description: Corrections that appeared in print on Wednesday, May 20, 2026.

item.title: Conservative Legislator Wins Republican Nomination for Oregon Governor
item.description: Christine Drazan’s victory in the Republican primary sets up a rematch with Gov. Tina Kotek, a Democrat, in November.
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin

media:content.media:credit: Jordan Gale for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Christine Drazan, a Republican state senator, won the state’s Republican primary for governor Tuesday.

item.title: Quote of the Day: Ebola Raged in Congo for Weeks Before Alarm
item.description: Quotation of the Day for Wednesday, May 20, 2026.

item.title: Idaho’s Republican Governor Wins Primary in Race for a Third Term
item.description: Gov. Brad Little will be heavily favored in a general election contest against an independent former state Supreme Court justice and a Democrat vowing to undo conservative policies.
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin

media:content.media:credit: Rebecca Noble/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Brad Little could become the third Idaho governor to serve three consecutive terms.

item.title: Trump Crushes Republican Dissent: 8 Takeaways From Tuesday’s Primaries
item.description: President Trump unseated Representative Thomas Massie, a top Republican critic in Congress, and also got his way in other primary contests.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein

media:content.media:credit: Michael Swensen for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Representative Thomas Massie of Kentucky ran a robust campaign against his Trump-backed challenger but was defeated on Tuesday night.

item.title: Senate Primary in Alabama to Replace Tommy Tuberville Heads to Runoff
item.description: In the race to replace Senator Tommy Tuberville, Representative Barry Moore was leading a multicandidate field that included Jared Hudson, a former Navy SEAL, and Steve Marshall, the state attorney general.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher

media:content.media:credit: Associated Press
media:content.media:description: From left, Jared Hudson, Rep. Barry Moore, center and Steve Marshall.

item.title: Former Atlanta Mayor Wins Democratic Primary for Georgia Governor
item.description: Keisha Lance Bottoms will be the Democratic standard-bearer, despite worries about her single term as mayor of the state’s biggest city.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas

media:content.media:credit: Alyssa Pointer/Reuters
media:content.media:description: Keisha Lance Bottoms greeted volunteers at a campaign phone bank in Atlanta on Tuesday.

item.title: Is This the End of Close Elections?
item.description: Rampant gerrymandering and intense partisan sorting have shrunk the number of competitive House seats to a record-low. The political scientist Lee Drutman explains how on “The Ezra Klein Show.”
item.dc:creator: ‘The Ezra Klein Show’

item.title: NYT Crossword Answers: May 20, 2026
item.description: Kathleen Duncan’s puzzle keeps us on our toes.
item.dc:creator: Isaac Aronow

media:content.media:credit: Will Warasila for The New York Times

item.title: Bob Brooks, Labor Leader Backed by Top Democrats, Wins Pennsylvania House Primary
item.description: Mr. Brooks will face Representative Ryan Mackenzie, a Republican, in the fall. He earned endorsements from across his party, including Senator Bernie Sanders of Vermont and Gov. Josh Shapiro of Pennsylvania.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning and Taylor Robinson

media:content.media:credit: Michelle Gustafson for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Bob Brooks arriving at his watch party on election night at a bar in Bethlehem, Pa.

item.title: Mike Collins and Derek Dooley Head to G.O.P. Senate Runoff in Georgia
item.description: Mr. Collins and Mr. Dooley are set to face each other on June 16 as they compete for the chance to challenge Senator Jon Ossoff, a popular Democrat.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk and Sean Keenan

media:content.media:credit: Audra Melton for The New York Times; Daniel Varnado/Atlanta Journal-Constitution, via AP
media:content.media:description: Representative Mike Collins and Derek Dooley.

item.title: A Tuberville-Jones Rematch Is Set for the Alabama Governor Race
item.description: Senator Tommy Tuberville, a Republican, will face the Democrat that he defeated in 2020, Doug Jones, after both easily won their primaries.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane

media:content.media:credit: Bob Miller for The New York Times

item.title: Republican Primary for Georgia Governor Will Go to a Runoff
item.description: The state’s lieutenant governor, Burt Jones, will face a billionaire health care executive, Rick Jackson, on June 16 to decide the party’s standard-bearer to succeed Gov. Brian Kemp.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas
item.category: Georgia Criminal Case Against Trump (2020 Election)

media:content.media:credit: Arvin Temkar/Atlanta Journal-Constitution, via Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Rick Jackson, left, and Burt Jones are the remaining Republicans vying to be the party’s candidate for governor of Georgia.

item.title: ICE Makes Courthouse Arrest Despite Judge’s Order
item.description: A Honduran man was taken into custody on Tuesday in New York City, which his lawyers said violated a prohibition imposed a day earlier. Hours later, the man was released.
item.dc:creator: Jonah E. Bromwich and Benjamin Weiser

media:content.media:credit: Madison Swart for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Mask-wearing federal agents had become a regular presence in the hallways of 26 Federal Plaza in Lower Manhattan.

item.title: Dallas Fort Worth Airport Cancels Hundreds of Flights as Storms Hit Texas
item.description: Much of the state is expected to face heavy rain for the rest of the week.
item.dc:creator: Erin McCann and Mark Walker

media:content.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Kentucky Had an Easy Choice Between Massie and Trump
item.description: The president wants a loyal Republican Party — even if the country might not.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Cottle

media:content.media:credit: Carolyn Kaster/Associated Press

item.title: Alabama’s Republican Senate Primary: Key Regions to Watch
item.description: Barry Moore has led in numerous polls, but a three-way race is likely to advance to a runoff.
item.dc:creator: Alex Lemonides

media:content.media:credit: Michael Brochstein/Sipa USA, via Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Representative Barry Moore is a candidate in the Republican Primary for United States Senate in Alabama.

item.title: 2 Die at Troubled Rikers Jail Complex in 24 Hours
item.description: The deaths, of a 40-year-old man and a 41-year-old woman, bring to four the number of fatalities at Rikers Island this year.
item.dc:creator: Ed Shanahan
item.category: Office of the Chief Medical Examiner of the City of New York

media:content.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Officials are investigating the deaths of Umais Khan and Rajpattie Ramkellawan, both of whom were in jail awaiting trial.

item.title: Thomas Massie Loses Kentucky House Primary to Trump-Backed Ed Gallrein
item.description: Representative Thomas Massie, President Trump’s chief G.O.P. antagonist in the House, lost to Ed Gallrein, Mr. Trump’s handpicked candidate.
item.dc:creator: Robert Draper

media:content.media:credit: Michael Swensen for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Rep. Thomas Massie, President Trump’s most prominent Republican antagonist in Congress, was defeated in his primary by a Trump-backed challenger.

item.title: Sen. Gillibrand of New York Clashes With Sean Duffy Over Funding Freeze
item.description: Senator Kirsten Gillibrand, Democrat of New York, criticized the transportation secretary, Sean Duffy, for posting a video that bragged about withholding money from the state.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron

media:content.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Senator Kirsten Gillibrand, Democrat of New York, speaking to Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy in a Senate hearing at the Capitol on Tuesday.

item.title: Minnesota Becomes First State to Ban Prediction Markets
item.description: Like at least other 14 states, Minnesota has wrestled with how to regulate the popular prediction market sites. Federal officials sued the state over its new law.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra

media:content.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The Minnesota State Capitol in St. Paul. Legislators passed a law to make it illegal to host or advertise a prediction market site in Minnesota.

item.title: Early War Goal Was to Install Hard Line Former President as Iran’s Leader
item.description: An Israeli strike designed to free Mahmoud Ahmadinejad from house arrest in Tehran, U.S. officials said, was part of an effort to bring about regime change and put him in power.
item.dc:creator: Mark Mazzetti, Julian E. Barnes, Farnaz Fassihi and Ronen Bergman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Former President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad of Iran registering as a candidate in the presidential election in Tehran in 2024.

item.title: San Diego Mosque Shooting Victims Remembered as Heroes
item.description: The imam of the Islamic Center of San Diego and the city’s police chief said that the victims sacrificed their lives and prevented more people from being killed.
item.dc:creator: Jackeline Luna and McKinnon de Kuyper
item.category: Islamic Center of San Diego Shooting (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Arafat Barbakh/Reuters

item.title: What to Know About the Republican Senate Primary in Alabama
item.description: Three leading contenders, including a Trump-backed congressman, are aiming to replace Senator Tommy Tuberville. The winner will be favored in November.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk

media:content.media:credit: Mike Stewart/Associated Press; Amanda Andrade-Rhoades/Reuters
media:content.media:description: From left, Attorney General Steve Marshall, Jared Hudson and Representative Barry Moore are facing off in the Senate race in Alabama.

item.title: ‘Beaches’ to Close on Broadway Months Earlier Than Planned
item.description: A musical version of the 1980s tear-jerker will close months earlier than planned after opening in April to negative reviews and soft sales.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson

media:content.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: “Beaches,” which was rushed to Broadway with a modest cast, had been scheduled to run through Sept. 6.

item.title: With Trump’s Settlement, a Possible $100 Million I.R.S. Penalty Melts Away
item.description: The tax service argued that the Trump Organization tried to claim the same losses twice. The president said the audit was a “disgrace.”
item.dc:creator: Russ Buettner

media:content.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The audit dated back to a $72.9 million tax refund that Donald J. Trump claimed, and received, starting in about 2010.

item.title: Key Areas to Watch in Georgia’s Republican Senate Primary
item.description: Three candidates are jockeying for position in a race that is likely to result in a runoff.
item.dc:creator: Caroline Soler

media:content.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Voters casting ballots in Atlanta on Tuesday.

item.title: Andy Barr Wins Republican Primary for McConnell’s Seat in Kentucky
item.description: Mr. Barr, who serves in the House, consolidated support among Republicans in part by securing the endorsement of President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti

media:content.media:credit: Jon Cherry/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Representative Andy Barr’s win over Daniel Cameron, long seen as a protégé of Senator Mitch McConnell, amounts to a capstone to Mr. McConnell’s decades-long dominance in Kentucky politics.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About Mike Collins, Republican Senate Candidate in Georgia
item.description: The congressman is a Trump acolyte who has a history of incendiary social media posts.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk

media:content.media:credit: Miguel Martinez/Atlanta Journal-Constitution, via Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Rep. Mike Collins’s hard-line views on immigration are a central part of his campaign message.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About Derek Dooley, Republican Senate Candidate in Georgia
item.description: Mr. Dooley is a former football coach running in the Republican primary with the support of Gov. Brian Kemp.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk

media:content.media:credit: Alyssa Pointer/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Derek Dooley, who is supported in the Senate race by Gov. Brian Kemp.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About Buddy Carter, Republican Senate Candidate in Georgia
item.description: The congressman is a Trump acolyte who has found some common ground with Democrats on energy issues.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk

media:content.media:credit: Miguel Martinez/Atlanta Journal-Constitution, via Associated Press
media:content.media:description: “My hope is that he will endorse me at some point,” Representative Buddy Carter has said of President Trump.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About Jon Ossoff, Democratic Senate Candidate in Georgia
item.description: He is the youngest member of the Senate and once volunteered for the civil rights leader John Lewis’s congressional office.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk

media:content.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Sen. Jon Ossoff, Democrat of Georgia.

item.title: Rep. Thomas Massie, Frequent Trump Critic, Is Ousted in Kentucky Primary
item.description: Mr. Massie, a Republican who frequently clashed with President Trump, lost to a Trump-backed challenger. The race was a closely watched test of the president’s power to punish his critics.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

media:content.media:credit: Michael Swensen for The New York Times
